articles.title: Q6 — Caregiving support
content: How adequate is the caregiving support for the elder's health and dependency needs?

ul: **Significant gaps (2)** — caregiving support inconsistent or insufficient
**Severely inadequate (3)** — serious caregiving inadequacy affecting safety or functioning

head: Response
Operational definition
rows: Serious caregiving inadequacy affecting safety or functioning.

head: Score
Triggered response
Priority
Owner
Timeframe
rows: Family meeting to clarify caregiving responsibilities; identify primary + backup caregivers; document agreement
**Critical flag review.** Settlement-level caregiving plan; identify community caregiver if no family member; home-help linkage if budget permits

articles.title: Q6b — Caregiver burden [Sensitive]
content: How is the primary caregiver coping with caregiving demands? **Asked privately of the caregiver, not the elder.** Where possible, speak to the caregiver alone — even a few minutes outside the house.

ul: **Coping well (0)** — no significant strain; caregiver managing demands
**Mild strain (1)** — manageable but tiring; some impact on caregiver's daily life
**Moderate strain (2)** — sleep loss, health affected, struggling to manage
**Severe burnout (3)** — caregiver showing exhaustion, resentment, or risk of breakdown
**Not applicable (NA)** — elder lives alone or has no identified primary caregiver

content: Score **3 (severe burnout)** is a critical risk flag → automatic Profound Need + respite within 14 days.

A daughter-in-law caring for a bed-ridden elder while raising children is at extreme burnout risk. An aged spouse caring for another aged spouse is similarly vulnerable. Look for fatigue, weight loss, irritability, sleep deprivation in the caregiver.

articles.title: Q6b — Operational definitions
content: Burnout precedes neglect. Q6 measures caregiving adequacy from the elder's side; Q6b adds the caregiver's voice. Catching strain early enables respite before harm.

head: Response
Operational definition
rows: No significant strain reported; caregiver managing demands.
Manageable but tiring; some impact on caregiver's daily life.
Sleep loss, health affected, struggling to manage.
Caregiver showing exhaustion, resentment, or risk of breakdown.
Elder lives alone or has no identified primary caregiver.

head: Score
Triggered response
Priority
Owner
Timeframe
rows: Caregiver support group enrolment; respite arrangement (a few hours per week); peer connection to other caregivers
**Critical flag — see §11.1 protocol.** Respite within 14 days; counselling referral; family task-sharing meeting

articles.title: Q8b — IADL dependence
content: For how many of the following does the elder need help from another person? Tick each: cooking, shopping, managing money, managing medicines, transport / going out.

ul: **0 activities (0)** — fully independent in all IADLs
**1 activity (1)** — needs help with one activity

content: IADL covers higher-order tasks needed to live independently. Loss often precedes ADL loss (ADL = Q8a) and signals coming dependency.

articles.title: Q8b — IADL dependence: how to count

head: Score
Triggered response
Priority
Owner
Timeframe
rows: Community kitchen enrolment if cooking affected; medication management plan (family member or pill organiser); shopping support arrangement
Full meal support via community kitchen; medication and money management transferred to identified family member or settlement leader
